ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for braised pig heart as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared pork organ dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised pig heart is approximately 100g, equivalent to several slices of the organ meat as served in a hawker or home-cooked meal.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Pig Heart — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for braised pig heart (F06090001). All core macros and key micronutrients are taken from this canonical entry. Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked pork heart entry (NDB 17168) as HPB does not report saturated fat fractions separately.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; no GI testing applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Braised pig heart contains only 0.4g carbohydrate per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI testing is warranted or available for this food type. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0 (calculated as GI x carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = negligible). The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and confirms no GI entry exists for pig heart or equivalent organ meat, consistent with the negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (35mg/100g) with favourable potassium ratio (206mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Braised pig heart is low in sodium at 35mg/100g and provides 206mg potassium, yielding a strongly favourable Na:K ratio; however, patients should request minimal soy sauce in braising preparations as sauces can substantially increase sodium content, and the high cholesterol content (221mg/100g) warrants moderation in frequency for hypertensive patients with concurrent cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.4g/100g) and high protein (23.6g/100g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.4g carbohydrate per 100g and 23.6g protein, braised pig heart has negligible glycaemic impact and supports satiety and glycaemic stability; patients should ensure braising sauces do not contain significant added sugar, and should moderate intake frequency given the high cholesterol content (221mg/100g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (221mg/100g) from organ meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Braised pig heart contains 221mg cholesterol per 100g, which is elevated for a cardiovascular disease patient; while the fat content is moderate at 5.05g/100g and sodium is low, individuals with established CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit consumption frequency and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to mitigate cholesterol absorption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid (omega-6) content in pork organ meat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Braised pig heart is a pork organ meat containing arachidonic acid, an omega-6 fatty acid that amplifies inflammatory pathways; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption frequency and pair with anti-inflammatory foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised pig heart (红烧猪心) is a traditional Chinese and Singaporean dish prepared by slow-braising the pig's heart in a seasoned liquid typically containing soy sauce, aromatics such as garlic, ginger, and star anise, and water or stock. The result is a firm, dense, and tender organ meat with a savoury, slightly soy-infused flavour. Pig heart is a nutrient-dense organ meat, providing 23.6g protein and only 5.05g fat per 100g, making it a high-protein, moderate-calorie food at 148 kcal per 100g. It is notably rich in iron (5.83mg/100g), which is clinically significant for iron-deficiency anaemia prevention, and contains 221mg cholesterol per 100g, which warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.4g/100g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is very low at 35mg/100g in the base preparation, though braising sauces can significantly increase this. The dish is commonly found in Chinese hawker stalls, zi char restaurants, and home kitchens across Singapore and Southern China, often served sliced over rice or as part of a mixed offal platter. It is not suitable for halal or kosher diets.

Nutrient data is sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (canonical primary source). Multilingual naming satisfies Singaporean dish requirements with Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi coverage.
